content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

SUPERSEDED
IN PART
by Reg.18945
(see A-9B-0712)

January 21, 1992

Richard Nishite
3807 Robie Lee Way
Sacramento, CA  95821-3246

Re:  Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-91-568

Dear Mr. Nishite:

     This is in response to your letter requesting advice as a
designated employee of the Office of the Secretary of State with
respect to the new gift and honoraria restrictions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").1  Please note the Commission is
currently considering regulations which will interpret the new
gift and honoraria restrictions of the Act.  Consequently, the
analysis set forth in this letter is subject to change in the
future.

QUESTION

     As a designated employee of the Office of the Secretary of
State, may you accept a payment for accommodations and an
honoraria for speaking at a conference put on by the Pacific
Conference, a private nonprofit entity, with sponsorship from
private organizations?

CONCLUSION

     Payments for accommodations and an honorarium provided to you
from the Pacific Conference for speaking are exempted from the
gift limits and disclosure requirements of the Act, so long as the
Pacific Conference is not a disclose source under your agency's
conflict of interest code.


1    Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear in 2 California Code of Regulations.  Commission
Section 18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-91-568
Page 2


          However, if the sponsors of the conference knew at the time
their donations were made that the funds would be used to make
gifts to you, the payments would be a gift from the various
private sponsors of the conference.  Under such circumstances, if
the sponsors are disclosable sources, the payments would be
disclosable and subject to the gift restriction of the Act unless
a specific exception applies.

                              FACTS

          You are a designated employee of the Office of the Secretary
of State. As a designated employee you are subject to the
disclosure provisions of your agency's conflict of interest code,
as well as the provisions of the Act.

          You stated that you received free accommodations and a $1,000
honorarium for attending and speaking at the Pacific Conference.
You paid for your own travel to the site of the conference in
Maui, Hawaii. You were asked to speak on the term limits imposed
by Proposition 140, and in exchange, you received accommodations
from Sunday, December 8, 1991, to Friday December 13, 1991, in
addition to the honorarium.2  The purpose of the conference as a
whole was to promote interstate and international cooperation in
the Pacific Region.

          The Pacific Conference is a nonprofit organization which is
exempt from taxation under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal
Revenue Code.  However, there were also 20 private sponsors to the
event, many of which are private organizations.  You stated in our
telephone conversation of December 30, 1991 that you were
notified late to the list of speakers.  You stated that you did not believe
that the corporate sponsors knew that a portion of the funds would
be used to provide free accommodations and an honorarium to you at
the time they were providing donations to the Pacific Conference.

                              ANALYSIS

          The Act imposes certain obligations on public officials with
respect to the receipt of gifts.  The term "public official" is
broadly defined in the Act to include every natural person who is
a member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local
government agency.  (Section 82048; Regulation 18700.)  As a
designated employee of the Office of the Secretary of State, you
are a public official.

          The Act requires that every public official disclose all his
or her economic interests that could foreseeably be affected by
the exercise of the official's duties.  (Sections 81002(c), 87200-
87313.)  Section 87300 provides that every agency shall adopt and
promulgate a conflict of interest code which specifically

_______
2      The duration of your accommodations was clarified in our
telephone conversation of December 30, 1991.

Our File No. A-91-568
Page 3


enumerates the positions within the agency which involve the
making or participation in the making of decisions which may
foreseeably materially affect financial interests and provide the
specific types of investments, business positions, interests in
real property, and sources of income which are reportable by the
person who holds that position. (Section 87302.)

Moreover, Section 87100 prohibits any public official at any
level of state or local government from making, participating in
making or in any way attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official knows or has reason to know the official has a financial
interest. (Section 87100.) An official has a financial interest
in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally, on any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a
donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating $250 or more in value
provided to, received by, or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.
(Section 87103(e).)

And finally, effective January 1, 1991, the Legislature
enacted a series of statutes that restrict the receipt of gifts
and honoraria by public officials. The Act now provides:

     No member of a state board or commission, and
     no designated employee of a state agency, shall
     accept an honorarium from any source, if the member
     or employee would be required to report the receipt
     of income or gifts from that source on his or her
     statement of economic interest. This section shall
     not apply to any part-time member of the governing
     board of any public institution of higher
     education, unless the member is also an elected
     official.

                              Section 89503(a).

     No member of a state board or commission, and
     no designated employee of a state agency, shall
     accept gifts with a total value of more than two
     hundred fifty dollars ($250) in a calendar year
     from any single source, if the member or employee
     would be required to report the receipt of income
     or gifts from that source on his or her statement
     of economic interest. This paragraph shall not
     apply to any part-time member of the governing
     board of any public institution of higher
     education, unless the member is also an elected
     official.

                              Section 89505(a).

Our File No. A-91-568
Page 4


     You have asked about your responsibilities with respect to
payments you received in connection with a speech you made at the
Pacific Conference.  According to your letter, you received free
accommodations and a $1,000 honorarium from the Pacific
Conference. However, we note that there is a list of private
sponsors of the event, as well as the nonprofit entity itself.

     In determining the treatment of the payments you have
received it is first necessary to determine who is the source of
the payments. We have advised that under some circumstances it is
appropriate to pierce through one organization which appears to be
the source of a gift, to the actual donors of funds used to
provide the gift.

     The test we have applied in the past is that where the
private donors to a nonprofit entity for a conference know or have
reason to know at the time they make their donations that the
funds will be used to make a gift to specific public officials,
the donors are the true source of the payment.  (Donovan Advice
Letter, No. I-91-357; McCloskey Advice Letter, No. I-91-263.)
Thus, if the private sponsors knew a portion of their donations
would be provided to you for accommodations or an honorarium, the
Pacific Conference would be merely the intermediary of the payment
and the private sponsors would be treated as the true source of
the payment.

Disclosure and Gift Restrictions

     Both the gift limits of the Act, set forth above, and your
disclosure obligations are dependent on the source of the payment.
You stated that your conflict of interest code requires that you
report the following:

     (1)  All investment in, income from, or
business position with any business entity which
produces, markets or distributes voting machines,
or vote tabulating devices, or which produces,
markets, or distributes paper or paper products.

     (2)  Any income received directly or
indirectly from any lobbyist, employer of a
lobbyist, candidate for state office, elected state
official, political party official, or person
holding a business position with any campaign
committee.

     (3)  All investment in, income from, or
business position with any business entity which
manufactures, markets, distributes or leases
electronic data processing equipment, forms,
supplies, or services.

Our File No. A-91-568
Page 5


     If the source of your accommodations and honorarium (whoever
it may be) does not fall within one of the categories set forth
above, the payments you received will not be disclosable or
subject to any limits.

     If the sources are disclosable sources, the gift limits and
disclosure obligations do apply unless a specific exception
applies.

     1.  Necessary Accommodations

     Regulation 18728 provides:

          Free admission, food, beverages and similar
     nominal benefits provided to a filer at an event at
     which he or she makes a speech or participates in a
     panel or seminar, and reimbursement or advance for
     actual intrastate travel and for necessary
     accommodations provided directly in connection with
     the event are not gifts or income and need not be
     reported by the filer.

     Staff is currently advising that accommodations and free
admission, food, beverages and similar nominal benefits provided
at the speaking event which fall within Regulation 18728 are not
"payments" for purposes of the new gift and honoraria limits. We
have interpreted the language of the new provisions to be
consistent with Regulation 18728. (Koppes Advice Letter, No.
I-90-517.)

     Thus, where accommodations are provided in connection with a
speech or with participation in a panel or seminar consistent with
Regulation 18728, the accommodations need not be reported and are
not subject to the gift and honoraria limits of the Act.³

     However, what are "necessary" accommodations is not defined
in Regulation 18728. Since the enactment of the new gift limits
and honoraria ban of the Act, we have applied the definition of
"necessary accommodations" as set forth in Section 89506 of the
Act. This would include lodging and subsistence for the day
immediately preceding, the day of, and the day immediately
following the speech.

     2.  Other Accommodations

     All payments for accommodations in excess of the three days
permitted in Regulation 18728 and Section 89506 would continue to
be treated as gifts which are disclosable and potentially subject

_______________
3    The "necessary" accommodations exception in Regulation 18728
would apply whether or not we would pierce through the nonprofit
Pacific Conference to its sponsors.

Our File No. A-91-568
Page 6


to the limits. However, Section 89506 provides two exceptions
from the limits (not from disclosure) of gifts of travel.
(Section 89505(b); Section 89504(b)(1).) Section 89506 provides:

     (a) Payments, advances, or reimbursements,
for travel, including actual transportation and
related lodging and subsistence which is reasonably
related to a legislative or governmental purpose,
or to an issue of state, national, or international
public policy, is not prohibited or limited by this
chapter if either of the following apply:

                    * * *

     (2) The travel is provided by a
government, a governmental agency, a foreign
government, a governmental authority, a bona
fide public or private educational
institution, as defined in Section 203 of the
Revenue and Taxation Code, or a nonprofit
charitable or religious organization which is
exempt from taxation under Section 501(c)(3)
of the Internal Revenue Code, or by a person
domiciled outside the United States which
substantially satisfies the requirements for
tax exempt status under Section 501(c)(3) of
the Internal Revenue Code.

               Section 89506(a), emphasis
               added.

     a. Provided by Pacific Conference

     Clearly, the program described in your letter and
accompanying materials is reasonably related to an issue of state,
national, or international public policy pursuant to the exception
in Section 89506. The program is intended to enhance business
between the United States and Pacific Rim Countries. Thus, if the
Pacific Conference is the source of accommodations free outside
the three days, while
disclosable, would not be subject to the limits of the Act.
(Section 89506(a)(2).)

     b. Provided by Private Sponsors

     If the source of the additional accommodations is not a
nonprofit charitable or religious organization which is exempt
from taxation under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue
Code, the payments for the additional accommodations would fall
out of the exception in Section 89506(a)(2), and to the extent

Our File No. A-91-568
Page 7


that the value of the additional accommodations outside the three
days exempted under Section 89506(a)(1) was over $250 attributable
to a single source, you would be required to pay down the excess
within 30 days of receipt of the gift in order to comply with the
gift limits. (Section 82028(b)(2).)

For example, if the additional accommodations were worth
$1,000, and there were four disclosable sources which paid the
full amount, each would have provided $250 and you would not be
required to return any portion. If three entities paid the $1,000
you would be required to return the amount necessary to make the
gift $250 or less from each source.

Under your facts there are 20 listed private sponsors of the
event. It is unlikely that any one will have provided pro rata
more than $250 in gifts. Thus you may accept the gifts without
limit.

However, the gifts would be subject to disclosure.
Regulation 18726.6 provides:

A gift which is received from multiple donors
must be disclosed if the gift's value equals or
exceeds $50. The names of donors who individually
have given less than $50 need not be separately
disclosed; it is sufficient to describe in general
terms those who gave the gift. If, however, any
donor or his or her agent or intermediary gave $50
or more, his or her name must be disclosed.

Consequently, if no single person provided a gift to you of
$50 or more, it is sufficient to describe in general terms the
group that gave the gift.

3. Honoraria

As a designated employee, you are prohibited from accepting
an honorarium from any source you would be required to disclose.
However, according to Section 89505, the ban
only applies to payments from disclosable sources. You are not
restricted from accepting gifts and honoraria from persons other
than those whose disclosure is required under the agency's
conflict of interest code. (Fishburn Advice Letter, No. I-91-
463.)

II. Disqualification

Please be aware, however, that Section 87100, et seq., of the
Act requires that public officials who have received income or
gifts of $250 or more from a single source must disqualify
themselves from all governmental decisions that will foreseeably
and materially affect the source.

Our File No. A-91-568
Page 8


Consequently, if you determine that the nonprofit
organization was a source of the payment and the payment was
valued $250 or more, you will be required to disqualify yourself
if a decision should come before you which will have a foreseeable
and material financial effect on the nonprofit organization.
Similarly, if the sponsors are the source of the payments and any
sponsor's pro-rata share is $250 or more, you are required to
disqualify yourself if a decision should come before you which
will have a foreseeable and material financial effect on the
sponsor.

I trust this letter has addressed your concerns. Should you
have any further questions regarding this matter, please feel free
to contact me at (916) 322-5901.4

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWW:aa


4 Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

However, if the sponsors of the conference knew at the time their donations were made that the funds would be used to make gifts to you, the payments would be a gift from the various private sponsors of the conference. Under such circumstances, if the sponsors are disclosable sources, the payments would be disclosable and subject to the gift restriction of the Act unless a specific exception applies.

The Act imposes certain obligations on public officials with respect to the receipt of gifts. The term "public official" is broadly defined in the Act to include every natural person who is a member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local government agency. (Section 82048; Regulation 18700.) As a designated employee of the Office of the Secretary of State, you are a public official.

The Act requires that every public official disclose all his or her economic interests that could foreseeably be affected by the exercise of the official's duties. (Sections 81002(c), 87200-87313.) Section 87300 provides that every agency shall adopt and promulgate a conflict of interest code which specifically

2 The duration of your accommodations was clarified in our telephone conversation of December 30, 1991.

enumerates the positions within the agency which involve the making or participation in the making of decisions which may foreseeably materially affect financial interests and provide the specific types of investments, business positions, interests in real property, and sources of income which are reportable by the person who holds that position. (Section 87302.)

Moreover, Section 87100 prohibits any public official at any level of state or local government from making, participating in making or in any way attempting to use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official knows or has reason to know the official has a financial interest. (Section 87100.) An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating $250 or more in value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(e).)

And finally, effective January 1, 1991, the Legislature enacted a series of statutes that restrict the receipt of gifts and honoraria by public officials. The Act now provides:

No member of a state board or commission, and no designated employee of a state agency, shall accept an honorarium from any source, if the member or employee would be required to report the receipt of income or gifts from that source on his or her statement of economic interest. This section shall not apply to any part-time member of the governing board of any public institution of higher education, unless the member is also an elected official.

Section 89503(a).

No member of a state board or commission, and no designated employee of a state agency, shall accept gifts with a total value of more than two hundred fifty dollars ($250) in a calendar year from any single source, if the member or employee would be required to report the receipt of income or gifts from that source on his or her statement of economic interest. This paragraph shall not apply to any part-time member of the governing board of any public institution of higher education, unless the member is also an elected official.

Section 89505(a).

You have asked about your responsibilities with respect to payments you received in connection with a speech you made at the Pacific Conference. According to your letter, you received free accommodations and a $1,000 honorarium from the Pacific Conference. However, we note that there is a list of private sponsors of the event, as well as the nonprofit entity itself.

In determining the treatment of the payments you have received it is first necessary to determine who is the source of the payments. We have advised that under some circumstances it is appropriate to pierce through one organization which appears to be the source of a gift, to the actual donors of funds used to provide the gift.

The test we have applied in the past is that where the private donors to a nonprofit entity for a conference know or have reason to know at the time they make their donations that the funds will be used to make a gift to specific public officials, the donors are the true source of the payment. (Donovan Advice Letter, No. I-91-357; McCloskey Advice Letter, No. I-91-263.) Thus, if the private sponsors knew a portion of their donations would be provided to you for accommodations or an honorarium, the Pacific Conference would be merely the intermediary of the payment and the private sponsors would be treated as the true source of the payment.

Disclosure and Gift Restrictions

Both the gift limits of the Act, set forth above, and your disclosure obligations are dependent on the source of the payment. You stated that your conflict of interest code requires that you report the following:

(1) All investment in, income from, or business position with any business entity which produces, markets or distributes voting machines, or vote tabulating devices, or which produces, markets, or distributes paper or paper products.

(2) Any income received directly or indirectly from any lobbyist, employer of a lobbyist, candidate for state office, elected state official, political party official, or person holding a business position with any campaign committee.

(3) All investment in, income from, or business position with any business entity which manufactures, markets, distributes or leases electronic data processing equipment, forms, supplies, or services.

If the source of your accommodations and honorarium (whoever it may be) does not fall within one of the categories set forth above, the payments you received will not be disclosable or subject to any limits.

If the sources are disclosable sources, the gift limits and disclosure obligations do apply unless a specific exception applies.

1. Necessary Accommodations

Regulation 18728 provides:

Free admission, food, beverages and similar nominal benefits provided to a filer at an event at which he or she makes a speech or participates in a panel or seminar, and reimbursement or advance for actual intrastate travel and for necessary accommodations provided directly in connection with the event are not gifts or income and need not be reported by the filer.

Staff is currently advising that accommodations and free admission, food, beverages and similar nominal benefits provided at the speaking event which fall within Regulation 18728 are not "payments" for purposes of the new gift and honoraria limits. We have interpreted the language of the new provisions to be consistent with Regulation 18728. (Koppes Advice Letter, No. I-90-517.)

Thus, where accommodations are provided in connection with a speech or with participation in a panel or seminar consistent with Regulation 18728, the accommodations need not be reported and are not subject to the gift and honoraria limits of the Act.3

This would include lodging and subsistence for the day immediately preceding, the day of, and the day immediately following the speech.

2. Other Accommodations

All payments for accommodations in excess of the three days permitted in Regulation 18728 and Section 89506 would continue to be treated as gifts which are disclosable and potentially subject

3 The "necessary" accommodations exception in Regulation 18728 would apply whether or not we would pierce through the nonprofit Pacific Conference to its sponsors.

to the limits. However, Section 89506 provides two exceptions from the limits (not from disclosure) of gifts of travel. (Section 89505(b); Section 89504(b)(1).) Section 89506 provides:
